notes: Intel estimates Itanium 2 has approximately 30 to 100% better performance than Xeon in enterprise and technical applications
Intel plans to improve performance of Itanium by more than seven times over the next three years
Itanium improved incrementally but never achieved 7x improvement or market dominance; Intel discontinued Itanium in 2021
More than one thousand key business applications support Itanium as of 2003
Itanium implemented on more than 50 platforms
IA-32 Execution Layer for 32-bit Windows application emulation delivers 50-70% of native Itanium performance
Intel pledges to achieve cost parity between Xeon and Itanium lines
x86-64 extensions (pioneered by AMD64) made Itanium cost parity moot; Xeon became dominant server processor
24% of mainframe users now have Linux on their mainframes per Aberdeen survey
IBM increasingly discounting mainframe prices and offering capacity-on-demand flexibility to retain customers
Microsoft NetBench benchmark shows single Linux image on mainframe has 20% less throughput than Wintel
Wintel Web server performance at least 50% better than Linux on mainframe per Microsoft benchmark
Microsoft claims annual TCO of mainframes is 10-20 times greater than Wintel machine
32% of Unisys customers planned to standardize on single OS; of those, 56% chose Windows
Users migrated all 2900 COBOL programs and all end users over the course of 1.25 years
Micro Focus COBOL and DB2 allowed porting without recompilation
Users achieved double or more performance on same workload after migration to Intel-based platforms
One user cited cost/MIPS improvement of close to an order of magnitude vs mainframe
One user indicated annual cost of ownership with Wintel was one-third that of equivalent mainframes
Despite migration costs the payback period was three months
Cultural resistance was the main barrier to changeover cited by interviewees
No user expressed concerns about Intel solutions in any area except Windows security; reliability concerns proved unfounded
AMD 64-bit Opteron technology seen as less real-world-tested by interviewees
AMD64 architecture (x86-64) ultimately became the industry standard for 64-bit computing over Itanium
Survey comprised 98 mainframe user respondents
40% of respondents from organizations with over $1 billion in revenues
Respondent organizations averaged 5-6 mainframes
62% rated robustness (reliability/availability) as most important mainframe criterion
49% rated performance/scalability as most important mainframe criterion
60% of respondents satisfied with mainframes; 40% neutral or dissatisfied
Cost of ownership area of greatest concern (3% satisfied vs 49% concerned)
64% of mainframe users believe Itanium can handle mainframe-class workloads
Surround strategy most preferred approach for Itanium adoption (31% rated most likely)
Cost of ownership was main inducement for Itanium consideration (43% of respondents)
Migration risk was primary barrier to Itanium adoption (30% of respondents)
Technology roadmap risk was second barrier to Itanium adoption (26% of respondents)
66% indifferent between Xeon and Itanium; 24% prefer Itanium; 9% prefer Xeon
40% of mainframe workloads perceived suitable for Itanium
Large minority (30-40%) of mainframe users motivated to choose Itanium over more mainframes
Mainframe usage declined but migration went to x86-64 and cloud computing rather than Itanium
Aberdeen concluded surround/extend strategies preferred over migrate/replace for Itanium adoption
Enterprises did adopt Intel for new workloads (surround strategy) but via x86-64 not Itanium
Aberdeen concluded Itanium ready for mainframe-class workloads with major benefits in performance and cost
CPU-intensive applications (batch and technical/scientific) deliver largest immediate payback on Itanium vs mainframe
Linux on mainframe and AMD Opteron not viewed by interviewees as comparable in value-add to Itanium
